sentences.text: When the Germans were not satisfied with the finding of the White Russians, the Ukrainians and Lithuanians, because they knew exactly how many children are in the ghetto and how many people don't work.
Because we-- the Jewish people were registered.
We were naive people in those days, at the beginning of the war.
And they send in German troops.
The-- the-- the-- first of all, in the morning, the people had to go out of the ghetto to work outside the ghetto.
We were allowed to go out to work under military guard.

And then the Germans came inside the ghetto and went from house to house to pick up, according to their estimation, how many women are not working, how many children are in the ghetto, and how many sick people may be in the ghetto.
And they came to our block.
We stayed in a block, a three story block of flats or apartments, which the Russians built for the workers in nearby factories.
And as they came into our block-- block of flats, our apartment, we were hiding under the staircase, and they find us.
And they told us to go outside, to stay in the street.
And we came out, and we find some more children standing already there.
And they told us to march behind a certain officer.
And we went from one flat to another flat, because there were no locks.
And it's made specially.
The Jewish people designed inside it, they can go through the complete apartment building, right through, and the doors were never closed.
In case you have to run out of fire or emergency or similar things as happening now.
And we walked through one flat and another flat and picking up children, women, sick people.
And with German guards.
And we were walking on another floor, and I was actually the third one behind this first German who took all of us behind him, where-- where-- where to go from behind him, from flat to flat.
And we walked in to another apartment, and there was another small apartment.
And it was locked.
Usually, the apartment is completely open, no padlocks, nothing.
With electricity.
So the German tried to open the door.
It was locked.
So he ordered the German soldier breaking the door, and let's see who's there or see what's going on.
And the-- the soldier broke the door with his rifle, bayonet.
And we marched in behind this German.
And we walked one room, another room.
And there was a curtain closed.
He pushed the curtain aside, and there was lying a young lady in bed, covered up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was shaking, shivering unbelievable.
And he say to her, don't you know-- oh, no.
Sorry.
He said to her, what are you doing here?
She says, I'm very sick.
And they pulled off her blanket off her body, and there was a tiny baby sucking her breasts.
He tells to the guard, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he speaks to the guards, and they say to the Jewish girl in bed, don't you know it's forbidden to fall pregnant in the ghetto.
She didn't answer him.
He ordered the guard to pick up the baby and take the baby to the windowsill and smash his head.
The cries from the mother was unbelievable.
The time he smashed the baby's head, and blood was splashing around everywhere.
She was lying in bed with a tiny nightie, and they told her to get out.
And they took her in the street, and they shot her.
And we carried on to another block.
And the same similar stories happened.
To pick up the sick and the old, and women, pregnant women or women who didn't have a permit.
Then finally, we came to a certain place by the fence of the ghetto.
And there they were digging holes.
I figured out straightaway that I suppose they're going to make the ghetto smaller again.
And my mother say, Reuben, you see.
They're digging for us graves already.
I said, no, Ma.
They're making the ghetto smaller, seeing they're taking away so many people.
And they told us to sit down there on the side of the road, and trucks came.
And buses came, painted windows with music.
And they start taking the people to the buses.
And where to-- and they had to produce work permits or put any particular permits.
If you haven't got any permit at all, you didn't stand a chance.
At the time, we didn't know where the bus is taking those people, and the trucks.
And when it came our cue to go to the buses-- this was a truck, actually-- it was my late mother, my late brother, my late sister, and myself.
My elder sister went to work.
I think she worked outside the ghetto, in the laundry.
I think so.
Her name is Shoshana.
She's alive today.
She was the-- the-- the eldest of us.
And when we came to the truck, we showed our-- our [GERMAN], privileged people.
People who their husbands or their wives are working for-- essential work for the Germans.
I showed my permit.
He looked at it and said, all right.
Go to this side.
My brother showed the permit.
Go this side.
And my mother, and the little sister by her hand, Miriam.
And they parted them, and they took the sister and dumped her into the truck.
And my mother wanted to go in.
The cries from my sister was unbearable.
She said, mother, mother, come with me.
Please, come with me.
I looked up to her.
I said, Mom.
What can you help her?
You've got three children here.
You know that she's going.
So my mother looked at me.
She said, Reuben, maybe you're right.
She kissed my sister, and she came with us, Mom.
We went back to our apartment, our room in the ghetto, apartment.
Apartment.
So room in the block apartment.
We were sharing more place with more people.
And then in the evening, 6 o'clock came.
My-- my father and my sister came.
And we told them the bad news that they took away the sister.
Well, the-- the sadness was very bad.
Most-- we were most upset.
My late father, my mother.
All of us were upset.
We knew that they took them to the [INAUDIBLE], to [INAUDIBLE], and that they were liquidated in a terrible death.
[INAUDIBLE].
I didn't see it.
But the rumor came after a week or two weeks that they put the sick people and the children and the women between wood logs and set-- set them alight.
And that's what happened to the children of [INAUDIBLE].
And life went on in the ghetto.
And from week from week, they used to take away-- just catch people in-- before the shops or in the ghetto itself, pick them up if they haven't got a permit.
Else wise, they used to arrest and take them to the police station, and you never saw them again.
Or they used to take them to different forests and shoot them and bury them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And life became very difficult in the ghetto, particular when the German front got harder for the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Food supplies in the ghetto barely came.
Meat, we used to get once in two weeks, 100 grams horse meat brought from the front lines.
Shot horses.
Potatoes, very scarce.
Bread, very little and flour, very little.
Sugar and other amenities we never got.
Sickness became a disease in the ghetto.
People used to get sick, and they used to be hospitalized wherever possible.
There was-- the small ghetto had a hospital, and the big ghetto had a big hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one morning, I see they surrounded the small ghetto.
And my place wasn't far from the small ghetto.
And the connection between the-- the two ghettos was over a wooden bridge, like I saw a movie here in cinema, or whatever-- whatever was the name.
I can't remember.
And it reminded me of the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they surrounded this little ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I saw them taking out people, marching out of the ghetto towards the other side, to the-- to the forts.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all of a sudden, I hear an explosion and a big flame.
And I see the Jewish hospital is burning.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Afterwards, I find out that the Germans put in flammable material and detonated the hospital, and the doctors and the nurses and the assistants and the patients, everybody was blown up or burned alive.
And this ghetto was liquidated in a matter of days.
They took away everybody to the forts.
And nothing remained from the small ghetto.
They dismantled the bridge, and it became only the one ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And life carried on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people tried to avoid to go to the doctors in the big ghetto, because they-- they never knew if the hospital will be blown up again or whatever will happen.
And we suffered bitter frost in 1943.
It was very cold.
As I said previously, we dismantled any firewood, whatever possible was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the trouble started when the Germans find out that the Jewish people in the ghetto got infected with lice and other insects, parasites.

So they build it, this disinfectant [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unit, which consists-- consisted of hot showers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] steamed the clothing by the wall, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- but not gas chambers.
We didn't know about gas chambers in those days.
And every two weeks, we had to go to have a shower and de-lice our clothing that we used to wear.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I think typhoid broke out.
I'm not sure.
But some disease broke out in the ghetto.
But we were able to handle it under control.
People used to die from starvation, from disease, and from beating out from nowhere.
If you walk in the streets or-- or to go to work, the Germans would just kick you or beat you up with their batons or with their rifles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was dangerous even to go to work.
The people who worked on the airfields, on the airport for the Germans, used to be beaten up to death.
They had to march to work and back from work.
People used to die from starvation, exhaustion, and disease.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And life carried on.
The Lithuanian who used to supply food at the black market at the cemetery stopped coming in to do dealing with the Jewish people, because it was very dangerous for them.
The Germans realized there was something going on.
Maybe they caught a few Jewish people.
And they kept [INAUDIBLE] that there shouldn't be any dealing between the Jews and the non-Jews.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they put an order that everybody must report in a certain place in the ghetto.
It was 5 o'clock.
And we marched there to the ghett-- to the assembly place.
And the whole ghetto was assembled there.
And nobody went to work.
Nobody was-- was ordered to go to work.
The Germans arrived, and they made a selection, who is going right, and who is going left.
Who's going left, we found out afterwards, survived, remained in the ghetto.
Who were going left didn't come back, was liquidated on a certain fort or in a forest or whatever it is.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we came to the Germans and looked at us, we showed them our papers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one German say to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To me, you go to the left.
You go to the right.
And my father and my late mother begged the Germans.
Please, we are together.
And my husband, here is his papers, chief electrician, electrician for the broadcasting cooperation in Kaunas.
Whenever he used to go to work, he didn't go with a guard.
He had a special permit to go without the guard, he and another guy.
And they looked at me, and they said, right.
Go-- go to the left.
And in the evening, they let us go back to the ghetto.
These who went to the right had a terrible fate.
They were shot or burned, maybe both.
And the ghetto started to become smaller and smaller.
People had to vacate buildings, the houses.
The ghetto consisted of three blocks of flats and mostly houses.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But in the houses, didn't stay one family.
Maybe five families stayed.
Maybe people shared a room, two or three families.
Children, were not so many left already because they took away in the-- in the children's action, the women's, and so on.
And we survived this action.
Action means they come to liquidate people, to take away people.
And we came home, and we made hot water, maybe a piece of bread we still had at home.
I can't remember.
And I survived this agony too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very bad.
And we knew that now it was a method of liquidation.
And we find out there is-- there is a ghetto in Poland, in Warsaw.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I can't remember if we know that the Jewish people rose against the Germans in 1943.
I can't remember the day.
But we knew that it's going to be an uprising to stand against the Germans.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I find out that there is a youth organization, who they call themselves partisans, visiting the ghetto from time to time and taking away young people to be trained [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as-- as fighters against the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And after the war, I find out that my cousin-- I actually met him in Israel-- he was one of the chief partisan organizer in the ghetto.
And there was a Jewish fighting coalitions force, but they didn't stay in the ghetto.
They were hiding in the forest.
And they used to get supplies from some farmers and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the Germans still took the people to work at the airfield, in particular.

Whenever they used to march out, maybe 10,000, 15,000 people to the airfields, to use to repair the runways, to use to maintain the aircraft, to use to carry the bombs, to dig trenches, to drink anti-- to build anti-aircraft places in case there are bombs and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And life became bad in the ghetto, very bad.
And disease, people used to die in the street.
People were afraid to go to the hospitals.
The ghetto police became ruthless to Jewish people.
I want to tell you a small incident.
The Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gave an order [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that they know there is hideouts in the ghetto.
And they want to reward the Jewish police and the Jewish firemen.
My cousin was a fireman.
I just met him in Los Angeles.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
So the Germans said, if you will show us where the Jewish people are hiding-- hiding [INAUDIBLE]-- excuse me.
Where the Jewish people are hiding themselves, we will reward you with better food, and we will protect you against any-- anything if something should happen to the Jewish people in the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I remember a case, a Jewish policeman wanted to save himself and his family.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he didn't know-- he didn't know, this policeman, that his family is hiding in this particular hideout.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he told the Germans.
And he showed the Germans where the hideout is.
And the Germans took out all the people in the hideout, and they shot them.
And there was standing the Jewish policeman, who saw that his wife and one of his children got shot as well.

And in several cases like this was in the ghetto, the people were afraid to talk to each other about hideouts or about other things in case somebody would go and get a reward or get a special certificate, which will prolong his life for another day, another hour.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKS] And life got on in the ghetto.
People died every day.
One afternoon, Sunday afternoon, the German trucks were driving around the ghetto, with [INAUDIBLE], or loudspeakers, as you say now, that everybody must come to a certain assembly place.
We thought already they are going to make another selection.
A selection means that they're going to select the people who are to survive in the ghetto or to be sent out from the ghetto to be liquidated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we come to this big place.
And here they are still erecting a big pole and a platform.
And they're making-- they're building a hanging place.
How do you call it in English?
A gallows.
And here they bring the poor Jewish guy, handcuffed.
And they put him on the platform.
And everybody had to watch.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they put the rope on his neck, and they pulled the blocks or whatever it was under his legs, and he was hanged.
And the German made a speech through-- through a loudspeaker that this bloody, dirty Jew has been hanged because he shot a German officer.
And you people don't think you can start nonsense with us.
Anybody who tries anything of nonsense, not only one will be shot, but hundred for one.
If you're going to kill one German, we'll kill 100 Jewish people on the spot here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And apparently, this guy was a partisan, and he was caught.
If he killed a German or not, this is not to my knowledge.
Those things we don't speak.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was already 1943, beginning of '44.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And rumors came that the Russian were advancing on the front line.
And we knew that General [NON-ENGLISH] is stuck not far from Stalingrad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Germans became very strict and very aggressive and very sadistic.
People used to come back from work at the gate.
They used-- we used to call it the-- the [INAUDIBLE].
Everybody used to come in.
Some of them used to be searched in case they smuggled in a piece of bread, a potato, or whatever it is.
My cousin, which is in Los Angeles, [INAUDIBLE]-- they used to call her the chicken woman.
She always used to smuggle in live chickens.
[CHUCKLES] And one day, it happened that one chicken made a loud noise.
She let it run away, because the Germans, if they catch it on your body, a chicken or whatever it is, you don't stand a chance to be a survivor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people used to be scared to smuggle in things.
The Germans used to undress you and search you for a potato or a piece of baloney, or any-- any food stuff.
And if you're caught, they used to take you away, and they never saw you again.
We people never saw these people again.
And the Germans realized that since Stalingrad, they got surrendered so many soldiers.
And they can't take them out from the cold and frost and snow.
And food supplies didn't come to the Germans.
It was a bitter winter, 1943.
And the Germans had no mercy for us at all.
Even the Germans, the good Germans, so called.
While the Jewish people from the ghetto worked, didn't help him with nothing, not even a piece of bread any more.
Because the comrades at the lines suffered.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the ghetto still gets smaller and smaller and smaller by taking out people at random.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or people came from work back at the gate.
They used to reassemble them, used to come trucks, used to take them away.
They never came back.
And even those people who used to sell food for gold or diamonds but lived in the ghetto didn't want to trade with the Jewish people.
I actually worked in the ghetto a certain time for my mom's cousin, a lady.
She used to manufacture ladies' scarves.
She used to buy in the ghetto from the Jewish people linen, bed sheets.
We used to cut up, and my second cousin was a-- a-- a material dyer to make colors in material.
And they figured out how to make it.
I used to be the printer.
They used to cut it from a [INAUDIBLE], from soft paper or hard paper.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I used to paint them.
First of all, they used to make a complete color.
From white, all different colors; blue, pink, red, yellow.
They used to dye them in the yard.
He's a mechanical engineer-- a chemical engineer.
He survived the war.
And they used to sell it on the airfield.
There used to be a song in the ghetto [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about the [INAUDIBLE].
And they used to sing it.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
This was a song in the ghetto similar.
Maybe a few words, I made a mistake.
Thank you.
I'll--
